author: Tertullian
book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
Not the Lowliness of the Material, But the Dignity and Skill of the Maker, Must Be Remembered, in Gauging the Excellence of the Flesh. Christ Partook of Our Flesh.

ccel.title: ANF03. Latin Christianity: Its Founder, Tertullian

source.title: ANF03. Latin Christianity: Its Founder, Tertullian

title: Not the Lowliness of the Material, But the Dignity and Skill of the Maker, Must Be Remembered, in Gauging the Excellence of the Flesh. Christ Partook of Our Flesh.

source.title: ANF03. Latin Christianity: Its Founder, Tertullian

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: Let me therefore pursue the subject before me—if I can but succeed in vindicating for the flesh as much as was conferred on it by Him who made it, glorying as it even then was, because that poor paltry material, clay, found its way into the hands of God, whatever these were, happy enough at merely being touched by them.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: But why this glorying?

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: Was it that, without any further labour, the clay had instantly assumed its form at the touch of God?

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: The truth is, a great matter was in progress, out of which the creature under consideration was being fashioned.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: So often then does it receive honour, as often as it experiences the hands of God, when it is touched by them, and pulled, and drawn out, and moulded into shape.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: Imagine God wholly employed and absorbed in it—in His hand, His eye, His labour, His purpose, His wisdom, His providence, and above all, in His love, which was dictating the lineaments (of this creature).

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: For, whatever was the form and expression which was then given to the clay (by the Creator) Christ was in His thoughts as one day to become man, because the Word, too, was to be both clay and flesh, even as the earth was then.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: For so did the Father previously say to the Son: “Let us make man in our own image, after our likeness.”

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: And God made man, that is to say, the creature which He moulded and fashioned; after the image of God (in other words, of Christ) did He make him.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: And the Word was God also, who being in the image of God, “thought it not robbery to be equal to God.”

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: Thus, that clay which was even then putting on the image of Christ, who was to come in the flesh, was not only the work, but also the pledge and surety, of God.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: To what purpose is it to bandy about the name earth, as that of a sordid and grovelling element, with the view of tarnishing the origin of the flesh, when, even if any other material had been available for forming man, it would be requisite that the dignity of the Maker should be taken into consideration, who even by His selection of His material deemed it, and by His management made it, worthy?

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: The hand of Phidias forms the Olympian Jupiter of ivory; worship is given to the statue, and it is no longer regarded as a god formed out of a most silly animal, but as the world’s supreme Deity—not because of the bulk of the elephant, but on account of the renown of Phidias.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: Could not therefore the living God, the true God, purge away by His own operation whatever vileness might have accrued to His material, and heal it of all infirmity?

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: Or must this remain to show how much more nobly man could fabricate a god, than God could form a man?

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: Now, although the clay is offensive (for its poorness), it is now something else.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: What I possess is flesh, not earth, even although of the flesh it is said: “Dust thou art, and unto dust shalt thou return.”

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: In these words there is the mention of the origin, not a recalling of the substance.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: The privilege has been granted to the flesh to be nobler than its origin, and to have happiness aggrandized by the change wrought in it.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: Now, even gold is earth, because of the earth; but it remains earth no longer after it becomes gold, but is a far different substance, more splendid and more noble, though coming from a source which is comparatively faded and obscure.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: In like manner, it was quite allowable for God that He should clear the gold of our flesh from all the taints, as you deem them, of its native clay, by purging the original substance of its dross.
